[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] [[Category:Geology of Japan]] [[Category:Geologic formations]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11573686}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11573686]] Wikidata name: Ryukyuan limestone [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ryukyū Limestone''' (ryūkyū sekkai gan) is a [[:en:Limestone|Limestone]] [[:en:Stratigraphy|Stratigraphy]] widely distributed throughout the [[:en:Ryukyu Islands|Southwest Islands]]. It was formed during the [[:en:Pleistocene|Pleistocene]] by the action of [[:en:Coral reef|Coral Reefs]], and was named in 1925 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 14) by [[:en:Hisakatsu Yabe|Yabe Nagakatsu]] and {{ill|Hasawa Masashirō|ja|半沢正四郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasawa Masashirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Distribution == It is widely distributed from the central to the southern parts of the Southwest Islands, and in particular in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] it covers about 30 [[:en:Percentage|Percent]] of the land. Its maximum thickness reaches up to 150 [[:en:Metre|Meters]].
;Regions where Ryukyū Limestone is Exposed [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] : [[:en:Treasure Island|Takarajima]] (northern limit of distribution), [[:en:Kikaijima|Kikaijima]], [[:en:Okinoerabujima|Okinaraibu Island]], [[:en:Yoronjima|Yoron Island]] [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] : [[:en:Iejima|Iejima]], [[:en:Sesoko Island|Sesokojima]], {{ill|Kouri Island|ja|古宇利島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kouri Island|1=Kouri Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518163}}, [[:en:Tsuken Island|Tsukanezima]], [[:en:Aguni Island|Awakuni Island]], central and southern parts of {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} : [[:en:Kitadaitōjima|Kitadaitōjima]], [[:en:Minamidaitōjima|Minamidaitōjima]] : [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Irabu Island|Irabujima]], {{ill|Tarama Island|ja|多良間島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarama Island|1=Tarama Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q700885}}, [[:en:Minnajima|Minamijima (Tarama)]] : Southern parts of [[:en:Ishigaki Island|Ishigaki Island]], [[:en:Taketomi Island|Taketomishima]], [[:en:Kuroshima|Kuroshima]], [[:en:Hateruma|Hateruma Island]]
Because coral reefs form in shallow seas, the presence of Ryukyū Limestone indicates that these areas were near sea level at the time of formation. For example, near the [[:en:Kerama Islands|Kerama Islands]] between {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} and [[:en:Kume Island|Kumejima]], Ryukyū Limestone is found at about 80 meters below sea level, indicating that subsidence occurred in that region.<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selection|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2016-12-23|language=ja}}</ref>
== Topography == The Ryukyū Limestone forms a plateau that rises on top of the underlying {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, which is the basement rock of the Southwest Islands, and is surrounded by steep slopes. Numerous [[:en:Solutional cave|Karst Caves]], numbering in the hundreds such as {{ill|Gyokusendō|ja|玉泉洞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokusendō|1=Gyokusendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572695}}, are distributed throughout the region. Unlike typical limestone formations where depressions known as “{{ill|Doline|ja|ドリーネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シンクホール|1=doline|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q188734}}” are common, the Ryukyū Limestone area is characterized by many embankment-like hills.<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本の地形7 九州・南西諸島|publisher=Tokyo University Press|year=2001|author=町田洋 et al.|isbn=4-13-064717-2|language=ja}}</ref>
== Characteristics and Uses == [[File:Kinjocho ishidatami-michi Shuri Naha04n4272.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Kinjochō Ishidatami-michi]] – a stone-paved road made of Ryukyū Limestone]] Ryukyū Limestone contains numerous [[:en:Stoma|Pores]], which allow substantial infiltration of groundwater. At the boundary with the less permeable {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, many springs are found. In [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyakojima]], this property has been utilized in the construction of [[:en:Fukuzato Underground Dam|Fukuri Dam]] and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], various [[:en:Gusuku|Gusuku]], and imperial tombs like [[:en:Tamaudun|Gyokuryō]]. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Kikkō-bako]], as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of [[:en:Hermatypic coral|Reef-building Coral]]. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.
== Footnotes =
{{Reflist}}
== References = * Yamauchi Yutoshi (監修), edited by the Kyushu Branch of the Society of Soil Mechanics, 『九州・沖縄の特殊土』, {{ill|Kyushu University Press|ja|九州大学出版会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Press|1=Kyushu University Press|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370212}}, 1983. == Related Articles = * {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}

[[ja:琉球石灰岩]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11573686}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Geography of Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Geologic formations]] [[Category:Geology of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11573686]] Wikidata name: Ryukyuan limestone [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ryukyū Limestone''' (ryūkyū sekkai gan) is a [[:en:Limestone|Limestone]] [[:en:Stratigraphy|Stratigraphy]] widely distributed throughout the [[:en:Ryukyu Islands|Southwest Islands]]. It was formed during the [[:en:Pleistocene|Pleistocene]] by the action of [[:en:Coral reef|Coral Reefs]], and was named in 1925 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 14) by [[:en:Hisakatsu Yabe|Yabe Nagakatsu]] and {{ill|Hasawa Masashirō|ja|半沢正四郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasawa Masashirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Distribution == It is widely distributed from the central to the southern parts of the Southwest Islands, and in particular in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] it covers about 30 [[:en:Percentage|Percent]] of the land. Its maximum thickness reaches up to 150 [[:en:Metre|Meters]].
;Regions where Ryukyū Limestone is Exposed [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] : [[:en:Treasure Island|Takarajima]] (northern limit of distribution), [[:en:Kikaijima|Kikaijima]], [[:en:Okinoerabujima|Okinaraibu Island]], [[:en:Yoronjima|Yoron Island]] [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] : [[:en:Iejima|Iejima]], [[:en:Sesoko Island|Sesokojima]], {{ill|Kouri Island|ja|古宇利島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kouri Island|1=Kouri Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518163}}, [[:en:Tsuken Island|Tsukanezima]], [[:en:Aguni Island|Awakuni Island]], central and southern parts of {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} : [[:en:Kitadaitōjima|Kitadaitōjima]], [[:en:Minamidaitōjima|Minamidaitōjima]] : [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Irabu Island|Irabujima]], {{ill|Tarama Island|ja|多良間島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarama Island|1=Tarama Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q700885}}, [[:en:Minnajima|Minamijima (Tarama)]] : Southern parts of [[:en:Ishigaki Island|Ishigaki Island]], [[:en:Taketomi Island|Taketomishima]], [[:en:Kuroshima|Kuroshima]], [[:en:Hateruma|Hateruma Island]]
Because coral reefs form in shallow seas, the presence of Ryukyū Limestone indicates that these areas were near sea level at the time of formation. For example, near the [[:en:Kerama Islands|Kerama Islands]] between {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} and [[:en:Kume Island|Kumejima]], Ryukyū Limestone is found at about 80 meters below sea level, indicating that subsidence occurred in that region.<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selection|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2016-12-23|language=ja}}</ref>
== Topography == The Ryukyū Limestone forms a plateau that rises on top of the underlying {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, which is the basement rock of the Southwest Islands, and is surrounded by steep slopes. Numerous [[:en:Solutional cave|Karst Caves]], numbering in the hundreds such as {{ill|Gyokusendō|ja|玉泉洞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokusendō|1=Gyokusendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572695}}, are distributed throughout the region. Unlike typical limestone formations where depressions known as “{{ill|Doline|ja|ドリーネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シンクホール|1=doline|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q188734}}” are common, the Ryukyū Limestone area is characterized by many embankment-like hills.<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本の地形7 九州・南西諸島|publisher=Tokyo University Press|year=2001|author=町田洋 et al.|isbn=4-13-064717-2|language=ja}}</ref>
== Characteristics and Uses == [[File:Kinjocho ishidatami-michi Shuri Naha04n4272.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Kinjochō Ishidatami-michi]] – a stone-paved road made of Ryukyū Limestone]] Ryukyū Limestone contains numerous [[:en:Stoma|Pores]], which allow substantial infiltration of groundwater. At the boundary with the less permeable {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, many springs are found. In [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyakojima]], this property has been utilized in the construction of [[:en:Fukuzato Underground Dam|Fukuri Dam]] and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], various [[:en:Gusuku|Gusuku]], and imperial tombs like [[:en:Tamaudun|Gyokuryō]]. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Kikkō-bako]], as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of [[:en:Hermatypic coral|Reef-building Coral]]. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.
== Footnotes =

For example, near the {{ill|Kerama Islands|en|Kerama Islands|WD=Q198422|lt=Kerama Islands}} between {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} and {{ill|Kume Island|en|Kume Island|WD=Q247515|lt=Kumejima}}, Ryukyū Limestone is found at about 80 meters below sea level, indicating that subsidence occurred in that region.<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selection|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2016-12-23|language=ja}}</ref>

At the boundary with the less permeable {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, many springs are found. In {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima}}, this property has been utilized in the construction of {{ill|Fukuzato Underground Dam|en|Fukuzato Underground Dam|WD=Q5507764|lt=Fukuri Dam}} and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}}, various {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku}}, and imperial tombs like {{ill|Tamaudun|en|Tamaudun|WD=Q1186324|lt=Gyokuryō}}. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and {{ill|Turtleback tomb|en|Turtleback tomb|WD=Q7856466|lt=Kikkō-bako}}, as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of {{ill|Hermatypic coral|en|Hermatypic coral|WD=Q1057485|lt=Reef-building Coral}}. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.
== Footnotes =
{{Reflist}}
== References = * Yamauchi Yutoshi (監修), edited by the Kyushu Branch of the Society of Soil Mechanics, 『九州・沖縄の特殊土』, {{ill|Kyushu University Press|ja|九州大学出版会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Press|1=Kyushu University Press|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370212}}, 1983. == Related Articles = * {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11573686]] Wikidata name: Ryukyuan limestone [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ryukyū Limestone''' (ryūkyū sekkai gan) is a {{ill|Limestone|en|Limestone|WD=Q23757|lt=Limestone}} {{ill|Stratigraphy|en|Stratigraphy|WD=Q134783|lt=Stratigraphy}} widely distributed throughout the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|en|Ryukyu Islands|WD=Q6897416|lt=Southwest Islands}}. It was formed during the {{ill|Pleistocene|en|Pleistocene|WD=Q25546|lt=Pleistocene}} by the action of {{ill|Coral reef|en|Coral reef|WD=Q11292|lt=Coral Reefs}}, and was named in 1925 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 14) by {{ill|Hisakatsu Yabe|en|Hisakatsu Yabe|WD=Q11584319|lt=Yabe Nagakatsu}} and {{ill|Hasawa Masashirō|ja|半沢正四郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasawa Masashirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Distribution == It is widely distributed from the central to the southern parts of the Southwest Islands, and in particular in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}} it covers about 30 {{ill|Percentage|en|Percentage|WD=Q11229|lt=Percent}} of the land. Its maximum thickness reaches up to 150 {{ill|Metre|en|Metre|WD=Q11573|lt=Meters}}.
;Regions where Ryukyū Limestone is Exposed {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} : {{ill|Treasure Island|en|Treasure Island|WD=Q185118|lt=Takarajima}} (northern limit of distribution), {{ill|Kikaijima|en|Kikaijima|WD=Q711507|lt=Kikaijima}}, {{ill|Okinoerabujima|en|Okinoerabujima|WD=Q94736|lt=Okinaraibu Island}}, {{ill|Yoronjima|en|Yoronjima|WD=Q94762|lt=Yoron Island}} {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}} : {{ill|Iejima|en|Iejima|WD=Q328533|lt=Iejima}}, {{ill|Sesoko Island|en|Sesoko Island|WD=Q137962|lt=Sesokojima}}, {{ill|Kouri Island|ja|古宇利島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kouri Island|1=Kouri Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518163}}, {{ill|Tsuken Island|en|Tsuken Island|WD=Q4518364|lt=Tsukanezima}}, {{ill|Aguni Island|en|Aguni Island|WD=Q398636|lt=Awakuni Island}}, central and southern parts of {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} : {{ill|Kitadaitōjima|en|Kitadaitōjima|WD=Q1111504|lt=Kitadaitōjima}}, {{ill|Minamidaitōjima|en|Minamidaitōjima|WD=Q1204188|lt=Minamidaitōjima}} : {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima}}, {{ill|Irabu Island|en|Irabu Island|WD=Q187366|lt=Irabujima}}, {{ill|Tarama Island|ja|多良間島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarama Island|1=Tarama Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q700885}}, [[:en:Minnajima|Minamijima (Tarama)]] : Southern parts of {{ill|Ishigaki Island|en|Ishigaki Island|WD=Q714339|lt=Ishigaki Island}}, {{ill|Taketomi Island|en|Taketomi Island|WD=Q474560|lt=Taketomishima}}, {{ill|Kuroshima|en|Kuroshima|WD=Q1207339|lt=Kuroshima}}, {{ill|Hateruma|en|Hateruma|WD=Q697600|lt=Hateruma Island}}
Because coral reefs form in shallow seas, the presence of Ryukyū Limestone indicates that these areas were near sea level at the time of formation. For example, near the {{ill|Kerama Islands|en|Kerama Islands|WD=Q198422|lt=Kerama Islands}} between {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} and {{ill|Kume Island|en|Kume Island|WD=Q247515|lt=Kumejima}}, Ryukyū Limestone is found at about 80 meters below sea level, indicating that subsidence occurred in that region.<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selection|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2016-12-23|language=ja}}</ref>
== Topography == The Ryukyū Limestone forms a plateau that rises on top of the underlying {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, which is the basement rock of the Southwest Islands, and is surrounded by steep slopes. Numerous {{ill|Solutional cave|en|Solutional cave|WD=Q7558985|lt=Karst Caves}}, numbering in the hundreds such as {{ill|Gyokusendō|ja|玉泉洞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokusendō|1=Gyokusendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572695}}, are distributed throughout the region. Unlike typical limestone formations where depressions known as “{{ill|Doline|ja|ドリーネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シンクホール|1=doline|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q188734}}” are common, the Ryukyū Limestone area is characterized by many embankment-like hills.<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本の地形7 九州・南西諸島|publisher=Tokyo University Press|year=2001|author=町田洋 et al.|isbn=4-13-064717-2|language=ja}}</ref>
== Characteristics and Uses == [[File:Kinjocho ishidatami-michi Shuri Naha04n4272.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Kinjochō Ishidatami-michi]] – a stone-paved road made of Ryukyū Limestone]] Ryukyū Limestone contains numerous {{ill|Stoma|en|Stoma|WD=Q216635|lt=Pores}}, which allow substantial infiltration of groundwater. At the boundary with the less permeable {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, many springs are found. In {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima}}, this property has been utilized in the construction of {{ill|Fukuzato Underground Dam|en|Fukuzato Underground Dam|WD=Q5507764|lt=Fukuri Dam}} and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}}, various {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku}}, and imperial tombs like {{ill|Tamaudun|en|Tamaudun|WD=Q1186324|lt=Gyokuryō}}. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and {{ill|Turtleback tomb|en|Turtleback tomb|WD=Q7856466|lt=Kikkō-bako}}, as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of {{ill|Hermatypic coral|en|Hermatypic coral|WD=Q1057485|lt=Reef-building Coral}}. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.

It was formed during the {{ill|Pleistocene|en|Pleistocene|WD=Q25546|lt=Pleistocene}} by the action of {{ill|Coral reef|en|Coral reef|WD=Q11292|lt=Coral Reefs}}, and was named in 1925 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 14) by {{ill|Hisakatsu Yabe|en|Hisakatsu Yabe|WD=Q11584319|lt=Yabe Nagakatsu}} and {{ill|Hasawa Masashirō|ja|半沢正四郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasawa Masashirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Distribution == It is widely distributed from the central to the southern parts of the Southwest Islands, and in particular in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}} it covers about 30 {{ill|Percentage|en|Percentage|WD=Q11229|lt=Percent}} of the land. Its maximum thickness reaches up to 150 {{ill|Metre|en|Metre|WD=Q11573|lt=Meters}}.
;Regions where Ryukyū Limestone is Exposed {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} : {{ill|Treasure Island|en|Treasure Island|WD=Q185118|lt=Takarajima}} (northern limit of distribution), {{ill|Kikaijima|en|Kikaijima|WD=Q711507|lt=Kikaijima}}, {{ill|Okinoerabujima|en|Okinoerabujima|WD=Q94736|lt=Okinaraibu Island}}, {{ill|Yoronjima|en|Yoronjima|WD=Q94762|lt=Yoron Island}} {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}} : {{ill|Iejima|en|Iejima|WD=Q328533|lt=Iejima}}, {{ill|Sesoko Island|en|Sesoko Island|WD=Q137962|lt=Sesokojima}}, {{ill|Kouri Island|ja|古宇利島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kouri Island|1=Kouri Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518163}}, {{ill|Tsuken Island|en|Tsuken Island|WD=Q4518364|lt=Tsukanezima}}, {{ill|Aguni Island|en|Aguni Island|WD=Q398636|lt=Awakuni Island}}, central and southern parts of {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} : {{ill|Kitadaitōjima|en|Kitadaitōjima|WD=Q1111504|lt=Kitadaitōjima}}, {{ill|Minamidaitōjima|en|Minamidaitōjima|WD=Q1204188|lt=Minamidaitōjima}} : {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima}}, {{ill|Irabu Island|en|Irabu Island|WD=Q187366|lt=Irabujima}}, {{ill|Tarama Island|ja|多良間島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarama Island|1=Tarama Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q700885}}, [[:en:Minnajima|Minamijima (Tarama)]] : Southern parts of {{ill|Ishigaki Island|en|Ishigaki Island|WD=Q714339|lt=Ishigaki Island}}, {{ill|Taketomi Island|en|Taketomi Island|WD=Q474560|lt=Taketomishima}}, {{ill|Kuroshima|en|Kuroshima|WD=Q1207339|lt=Kuroshima}}, {{ill|Hateruma|en|Hateruma|WD=Q697600|lt=Hateruma Island}}
Because coral reefs form in shallow seas, the presence of Ryukyū Limestone indicates that these areas were near sea level at the time of formation. For example, near the {{ill|Kerama Islands|en|Kerama Islands|WD=Q198422|lt=Kerama Islands}} between {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} and {{ill|Kume Island|en|Kume Island|WD=Q247515|lt=Kumejima}}, Ryukyū Limestone is found at about 80 meters below sea level, indicating that subsidence occurred in that region.<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selection|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2016-12-23|language=ja}}</ref>

Numerous {{ill|Solutional cave|en|Solutional cave|WD=Q7558985|lt=Karst Caves}}, numbering in the hundreds such as {{ill|Gyokusendō|ja|玉泉洞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokusendō|1=Gyokusendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572695}}, are distributed throughout the region. Unlike typical limestone formations where depressions known as “{{ill|Doline|ja|ドリーネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シンクホール|1=doline|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q188734}}” are common, the Ryukyū Limestone area is characterized by many embankment-like hills.<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本の地形7 九州・南西諸島|publisher=Tokyo University Press|year=2001|author=町田洋 et al.|isbn=4-13-064717-2|language=ja}}</ref>

At the boundary with the less permeable {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, many springs are found. In {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima}}, this property has been utilized in the construction of {{ill|Fukuzato Underground Dam|en|Fukuzato Underground Dam|WD=Q5507764|lt=Fukuri Dam}} and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}}, various {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku}}, and imperial tombs like {{ill|Tamaudun|en|Tamaudun|WD=Q1186324|lt=Gyokuryō}}. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and {{ill|Turtleback tomb|en|Turtleback tomb|WD=Q7856466|lt=Kikkō-bako}}, as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of {{ill|Hermatypic coral|en|Hermatypic coral|WD=Q1057485|lt=Reef-building Coral}}. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:琉球石灰岩]]
'''Ryukyū Limestone''' (ryūkyū sekkai gan) is a {{ill|Limestone|en|Limestone|WD=Q23757|lt=Limestone}} {{ill|Stratigraphy|en|Stratigraphy|WD=Q134783|lt=Stratigraphy}} widely distributed throughout the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|en|Ryukyu Islands|WD=Q6897416|lt=Southwest Islands}}. It was formed during the {{ill|Pleistocene|en|Pleistocene|WD=Q25546|lt=Pleistocene}} by the action of {{ill|Coral reef|en|Coral reef|WD=Q11292|lt=Coral Reefs}}, and was named in 1925 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 14) by {{ill|Hisakatsu Yabe|en|Hisakatsu Yabe|WD=Q11584319|lt=Yabe Nagakatsu}} and {{ill|Hasawa Masashirō|ja|半沢正四郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasawa Masashirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Distribution == It is widely distributed from the central to the southern parts of the Southwest Islands, and in particular in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}} it covers about 30 {{ill|Percentage|en|Percentage|WD=Q11229|lt=Percent}} of the land. Its maximum thickness reaches up to 150 {{ill|Metre|en|Metre|WD=Q11573|lt=Meters}}.
;Regions where Ryukyū Limestone is Exposed {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} : {{ill|Treasure Island|en|Treasure Island|WD=Q185118|lt=Takarajima}} (northern limit of distribution), {{ill|Kikaijima|en|Kikaijima|WD=Q711507|lt=Kikaijima}}, {{ill|Okinoerabujima|en|Okinoerabujima|WD=Q94736|lt=Okinaraibu Island}}, {{ill|Yoronjima|en|Yoronjima|WD=Q94762|lt=Yoron Island}} {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}} : {{ill|Iejima|en|Iejima|WD=Q328533|lt=Iejima}}, {{ill|Sesoko Island|en|Sesoko Island|WD=Q137962|lt=Sesokojima}}, {{ill|Kouri Island|ja|古宇利島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kouri Island|1=Kouri Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518163}}, {{ill|Tsuken Island|en|Tsuken Island|WD=Q4518364|lt=Tsukanezima}}, {{ill|Aguni Island|en|Aguni Island|WD=Q398636|lt=Awakuni Island}}, central and southern parts of {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} : {{ill|Kitadaitōjima|en|Kitadaitōjima|WD=Q1111504|lt=Kitadaitōjima}}, {{ill|Minamidaitōjima|en|Minamidaitōjima|WD=Q1204188|lt=Minamidaitōjima}} : {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima}}, {{ill|Irabu Island|en|Irabu Island|WD=Q187366|lt=Irabujima}}, {{ill|Tarama Island|ja|多良間島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarama Island|1=Tarama Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q700885}}, [[:en:Minnajima|Minamijima (Tarama)]] : Southern parts of {{ill|Ishigaki Island|en|Ishigaki Island|WD=Q714339|lt=Ishigaki Island}}, {{ill|Taketomi Island|en|Taketomi Island|WD=Q474560|lt=Taketomishima}}, {{ill|Kuroshima|en|Kuroshima|WD=Q1207339|lt=Kuroshima}}, {{ill|Hateruma|en|Hateruma|WD=Q697600|lt=Hateruma Island}}
Because coral reefs form in shallow seas, the presence of Ryukyū Limestone indicates that these areas were near sea level at the time of formation. For example, near the {{ill|Kerama Islands|en|Kerama Islands|WD=Q198422|lt=Kerama Islands}} between {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} and {{ill|Kume Island|en|Kume Island|WD=Q247515|lt=Kumejima}}, Ryukyū Limestone is found at about 80 meters below sea level, indicating that subsidence occurred in that region.<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selection|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2016-12-23|language=ja}}</ref>
== Topography == The Ryukyū Limestone forms a plateau that rises on top of the underlying {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, which is the basement rock of the Southwest Islands, and is surrounded by steep slopes. Numerous {{ill|Solutional cave|en|Solutional cave|WD=Q7558985|lt=Karst Caves}}, numbering in the hundreds such as {{ill|Gyokusendō|ja|玉泉洞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokusendō|1=Gyokusendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572695}}, are distributed throughout the region. Unlike typical limestone formations where depressions known as “{{ill|Doline|ja|ドリーネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シンクホール|1=doline|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q188734}}” are common, the Ryukyū Limestone area is characterized by many embankment-like hills.<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本の地形7 九州・南西諸島|publisher=Tokyo University Press|year=2001|author=町田洋 et al.|isbn=4-13-064717-2|language=ja}}</ref>
== Characteristics and Uses == [[File:Kinjocho ishidatami-michi Shuri Naha04n4272.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Kinjochō Ishidatami-michi]] – a stone-paved road made of Ryukyū Limestone]] Ryukyū Limestone contains numerous {{ill|Stoma|en|Stoma|WD=Q216635|lt=Pores}}, which allow substantial infiltration of groundwater. At the boundary with the less permeable {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, many springs are found. In {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima}}, this property has been utilized in the construction of {{ill|Fukuzato Underground Dam|en|Fukuzato Underground Dam|WD=Q5507764|lt=Fukuri Dam}} and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}}, various {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku}}, and imperial tombs like {{ill|Tamaudun|en|Tamaudun|WD=Q1186324|lt=Gyokuryō}}. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and {{ill|Turtleback tomb|en|Turtleback tomb|WD=Q7856466|lt=Kikkō-bako}}, as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of {{ill|Hermatypic coral|en|Hermatypic coral|WD=Q1057485|lt=Reef-building Coral}}. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.
== Footnotes =
{{Reflist}}
== References = * Yamauchi Yutoshi (監修), edited by the Kyushu Branch of the Society of Soil Mechanics, 『九州・沖縄の特殊土』, {{ill|Kyushu University Press|ja|九州大学出版会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Press|1=Kyushu University Press|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370212}}, 1983. == Related Articles = * {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Shimajiri Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}

{{ill|Kuroshima|en|Kuroshima|WD=Q1207339|lt=Kuroshima|1=Kuroshima}}, {{ill|Hateruma|en|Hateruma|WD=Q697600|lt=Hateruma Island|1=Hateruma}}
Because coral reefs form in shallow seas, the presence of Ryukyū Limestone indicates that these areas were near sea level at the time of formation. For example, near the {{ill|Kerama Islands|en|Kerama Islands|WD=Q198422|lt=Kerama Islands|1=Kerama Islands}} between {{ill|Okinawa Island|ja|沖縄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Island|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄本島|1=Okinawa Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q600614}} and {{ill|Kume Island|en|Kume Island|WD=Q247515|lt=Kumejima|1=Kume Island}}, Ryukyū Limestone is found at about 80 meters below sea level, indicating that subsidence occurred in that region.<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selection|publisher=Osaka Prefecture|accessdate=2016-12-23|language=ja}}</ref>
== Topography == The Ryukyū Limestone forms a plateau that rises on top of the underlying {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Q11476282|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, which is the basement rock of the Southwest Islands, and is surrounded by steep slopes. Numerous {{ill|Solutional cave|en|Solutional cave|WD=Q7558985|lt=Karst Caves|1=Solutional cave}}, numbering in the hundreds such as {{ill|Gyokusendō|ja|玉泉洞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokusendō|1=Gyokusendō Cave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572695}}, are distributed throughout the region. Unlike typical limestone formations where depressions known as “{{ill|Doline|ja|ドリーネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doline|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シンクホール|1=Sinkhole|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q188734}}” are common, the Ryukyū Limestone area is characterized by many embankment-like hills.<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本の地形7 九州・南西諸島|publisher=Tokyo University Press|year=2001|author=町田洋 et al.|isbn=4-13-064717-2|language=ja}}</ref>
== Characteristics and Uses == [[File:Kinjocho ishidatami-michi Shuri Naha04n4272.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Kinjochō Ishidatami-michi]] – a stone-paved road made of Ryukyū Limestone]] Ryukyū Limestone contains numerous {{ill|Stoma|en|Stoma|WD=Q216635|lt=Pores|1=Stoma}}, which allow substantial infiltration of groundwater. At the boundary with the less permeable {{ill|Shimajiri Mudstone|ja|島尻層泥岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimajiri Mudstone|1=Q11476282|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476282}}, many springs are found. In {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima|1=Miyako Island}}, this property has been utilized in the construction of {{ill|Fukuzato Underground Dam|en|Fukuzato Underground Dam|WD=Q5507764|lt=Fukuri Dam|1=Fukuzato Underground Dam}} and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}}, various {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku|1=Gusuku}}, and imperial tombs like {{ill|Tamaudun|en|Tamaudun|WD=Q1186324|lt=Gyokuryō|1=Tamaudun}}. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and {{ill|Turtleback tomb|en|Turtleback tomb|WD=Q7856466|lt=Kikkō-bako|1=Turtleback tomb}}, as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of {{ill|Hermatypic coral|en|Hermatypic coral|WD=Q1057485|lt=Reef-building Coral|1=Hermatypic coral}}. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.

'''Ryukyū Limestone''' (ryūkyū sekkai gan) is a {{ill|Limestone|en|Limestone|WD=Q23757|lt=Limestone|1=Limestone}} {{ill|Stratigraphy|en|Stratigraphy|WD=Q134783|lt=Stratigraphy|1=Stratigraphy}} widely distributed throughout the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|en|Ryukyu Islands|WD=Q6897416|lt=Southwest Islands|1=Ryukyu Islands}}. It was formed during the {{ill|Pleistocene|en|Pleistocene|WD=Q25546|lt=Pleistocene|1=Pleistocene}} by the action of {{ill|Coral reef|en|Coral reef|WD=Q11292|lt=Coral Reefs|1=Coral reef}}, and was named in 1925 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 14) by {{ill|Hisakatsu Yabe|en|Hisakatsu Yabe|WD=Q11584319|lt=Yabe Nagakatsu|1=Hisakatsu Yabe}} and {{ill|Hasawa Masashirō|ja|半沢正四郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasawa Masashirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Distribution == It is widely distributed from the central to the southern parts of the Southwest Islands, and in particular in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}} it covers about 30 {{ill|Percentage|en|Percentage|WD=Q11229|lt=Percent|1=Percentage}} of the land. Its maximum thickness reaches up to 150 {{ill|Metre|en|Metre|WD=Q11573|lt=Meters|1=Metre}}.
;Regions where Ryukyū Limestone is Exposed {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} : {{ill|Treasure Island|en|Treasure Because coral reefs form in shallow seas, the presence of Ryukyū Limestone indicates that these areas were near sea level at the time of formation. In {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyakojima|1=Miyako Island}}, this property has been utilized in the construction of {{ill|Fukuzato Underground Dam|en|Fukuzato Underground Dam|WD=Q5507764|lt=Fukuri Dam|1=Fukuzato Underground Dam}} and other underground dams.
Since ancient times in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Ryukyū Limestone has been used as a building material. It was employed in constructing stone-paved roads and stone walls surrounding houses, and was used in the construction of fortifications such as {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}}, various {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku|1=Gusuku}}, and imperial tombs like {{ill|Tamaudun|en|Tamaudun|WD=Q1186324|lt=Gyokuryō|1=Tamaudun}}. Today, it is still used as a building material for stone walls and {{ill|Turtleback tomb|en|Turtleback tomb|WD=Q7856466|lt=Kikkō-bako|1=Turtleback tomb}}, as well as being mixed with asphalt as aggregate for road paving. However, stone pavements made of Ryukyū Limestone become very slippery when wet.
== Types == The types of limestone vary according to the water depth of the sea in which they were formed. Because the substrate of the Southwest Islands has repeatedly experienced uplift and subsidence, and due to fluctuations in the sea level, various types of limestone can be observed in different regions and periods.<ref name="R01">{{Cite book|title=沖縄の自然 地形と地質|publisher=Hirugisha|year=1990|author=松田伸也 and Zenko Zemma|language=ja}}</ref> ; Coral Limestone : Limestone rich in fossils of {{ill|Hermatypic coral|en|Hermatypic coral|WD=Q1057485|lt=Reef-building Coral|1=Hermatypic coral}}. ; Calcareous Algae Nodular Limestone : Limestone abundant in {{ill|Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja|石灰藻球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calcareous Algae Nodules|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Cycloperrius Limestone : Limestone containing large numbers of foraminifera such as {{ill|Cycloperrius|ja|サイクロペリウス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cycloperrius|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Opacirina|ja|オパキュリナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opacirina|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. ; Clastic Limestone : Limestone containing many fragments of foraminifera, corals, algae, and bryozoans.
